card.indications: Treatment of selected seizure disorders and panic disorder. It may also be used short term for other severe anxiety-related conditions when prescribed.
card.mechanism: Enhances gamma-aminobutyric acid activity at GABA-A receptors, increasing inhibitory neurotransmission and reducing excessive neuronal firing and anxiety.
card.dosing: Start low and titrate gradually. Panic disorder commonly begins at 0.25 mg twice daily. Seizure treatment commonly begins with 0.5 mg three times daily in adults, with gradual adjustment. Older adults and patients with hepatic or respiratory risk generally need lower doses.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Allow orally disintegrating tablets to dissolve on the tongue using dry hands. Use on a consistent schedule for seizure control. Taper gradually; abrupt discontinuation may precipitate severe withdrawal or status epilepticus.
card.contraindications: Significant liver disease, acute narrow-angle glaucoma, or hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines. Use extreme caution with severe respiratory disease, sleep apnea, pregnancy, substance-use disorders, or concurrent opioids.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings address abuse, misuse, addiction, physical dependence, withdrawal, and profound sedation or respiratory depression with opioids. It can increase suicidal thoughts, impair driving, cause falls, and produce paradoxical agitation. Withdrawal may cause seizures.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, fatigue, ataxia, impaired coordination, memory problems, depression, and increased salivation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, coma, severe withdrawal seizures, status epilepticus, suicidal behavior, severe paradoxical agitation, blood dyscrasias, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Opioids, alcohol, sedating antihistamines, antipsychotics, sleep drugs, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory depression. CYP3A inhibitors may increase clonazepam exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor seizure frequency or panic symptoms, mental status, mood and suicide risk, respiratory rate, sedation, gait, falls, misuse, and signs of tolerance or dependence. Monitor liver function and CBC when clinically indicated.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Avoid alcohol, opioids, and unapproved sedatives. Do not drive until effects are known. Store securely and take only the prescribed amount. Seek help for slowed breathing, extreme sleepiness, suicidal thoughts, or worsening seizures.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if nausea occurs. Avoid alcohol completely. Maintain regular meals and hydration; excessive caffeine may worsen anxiety or sleep disturbance.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension. Certain extended-release products are used for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Other uses, such as withdrawal symptoms, pain, or hot flashes, depend on formulation and prescriber guidance.
card.mechanism: Stimulates central alpha2-adrenergic receptors, reducing sympathetic outflow from the brainstem. This lowers heart rate, peripheral vascular resistance, and blood pressure.
card.dosing: Hypertension commonly begins with 0.1 mg orally twice daily and is adjusted gradually. Transdermal patches deliver medication for 7 days and are selected by prescribed strength. Extended-release products use condition-specific schedules and are not interchangeable milligram for milligram with immediate-release tablets.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses consistently. Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Apply patches to clean, dry, hairless upper arm or torso skin, rotate sites, remove the old patch first, and replace every 7 days. Taper gradually over several days; abrupt withdrawal can cause severe rebound hypertension.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clonidine or patch components. Use caution with bradycardia, heart block, severe coronary or cerebrovascular disease, renal impairment, depression, and patients taking other rate-lowering drugs.
card.warnings: Abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension, nervousness, headache, tremor, and rarely stroke or death. Clonidine can cause severe bradycardia, hypotension, sedation, syncope, and conduction abnormalities. Patches may cause significant skin reactions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, drowsiness, dizziness, constipation, fatigue, headache, bradycardia, and patch-site redness or itching.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe rebound hypertension, heart block, profound bradycardia, syncope, severe hypotension, stroke, severe skin reaction, and CNS depression.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives and rate-lowering drugs increase hypotension or bradycardia. Alcohol and sedatives increase CNS depression. Tricyclic antidepressants may reduce the antihypertensive effect. If clonidine and a beta blocker are being stopped, the sequence must be clinician directed.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, orthostatic symptoms, sedation, adherence, patch site, renal function when indicated, and signs of rebound between doses or after missed therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Never stop suddenly or skip repeated doses. Check blood pressure and pulse if instructed. Rise slowly and avoid driving until alertness is known. Rotate patch sites and fold used patches together before safe disposal. Report fainting, a very slow pulse, chest pain, or severe headache.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration and fiber for dry mouth and constipation unless restricted. Avoid alcohol. Follow prescribed sodium guidance for hypertension.
